coredata.dc:title: Cu-MOF/N-doped GO nanocomposites modified screen-printed carbon electrode towards detection of 4-nitrophenol
coredata.dc:creator: Ambaye, Abera Demeke
Kefeni, Kebede Keterew
Kebede, Temesgen Girma
Ntsendwana, Bulelwa
Mishra, Shivani Bhardwaj
Nxumalo, Edward Ndumiso
coredata.dc:description: Metal-organic framework-based nanocomposites have recently received much attention in electrochemical sensors. Herein, copper-metal organic framework/nitrogen-doped graphene oxide (Cu-MOF/NGO) nanocomposites were synthesized using a solvothermal approach. The materials were characterized by field emission scanning electron microscopy/energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy, Brunauer–Emmett–Teller analysis, X-ray diffraction analysis, thermogravimetric analysis, and UV-visible spectroscopy. The Cu-MOF/NGO nanocomposites were applied in the modification of a screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) towards detection of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) using differential pulse voltammetry. Moreover, the electrode materials were characterized using Raman spectroscopy, atomic force microscopy, cyclic voltammetry, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. In ferricyanide and ferrocene redox probes, the Cu-MOF/NGO/SPCE showed improved current response and charge transfer kinetics. The Cu-MOF/NGO/SPCE showed a lower limit of detection (0.035 μmol L−1) and limit of quantification (0.116 μmol L−1) within a linear range of concentration (0.5–100 μmol L−1). The developed electrode demonstrated good analytical features of selectivity, repeatability, and reproducibility. The application of the Cu-MOF/NGO/SPCE was found to be successful in the determination of 4-NP in wastewater samples, with good recovery results in the range of 103.00 to 107.33%.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal-organic framework-based nanocomposites have recently received much attention in electrochemical sensors. Herein, copper-metal organic framework/nitrogen-doped graphene oxide (Cu-MOF/NGO) nanocomposites were synthesized using a solvothermal approach. The materials were characterized by field emission scanning electron microscopy/energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy, Brunauer–Emmett–Teller analysis, X-ray diffraction analysis, thermogravimetric analysis, and UV-visible spectroscopy. The Cu-MOF/NGO nanocomposites were applied in the modification of a screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) towards detection of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) using differential pulse voltammetry. Moreover, the electrode materials were characterized using Raman spectroscopy, atomic force microscopy, cyclic voltammetry, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. In ferricyanide and ferrocene redox probes, the Cu-MOF/NGO/SPCE showed improved current response and charge transfer kinetics. The Cu-MOF/NGO/SPCE showed a lower limit of detection (0.035 μmol L−1) and limit of quantification (0.116 μmol L−1) within a linear range of concentration (0.5–100 μmol L−1). The developed electrode demonstrated good analytical features of selectivity, repeatability, and reproducibility. The application of the Cu-MOF/NGO/SPCE was found to be successful in the determination of 4-NP in wastewater samples, with good recovery results in the range of 103.00 to 107.33%.

ce:section.ce:para: A phenolic derivative of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) is used for the production of pesticides, explosives, and pharmaceuticals [1]. The Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) of the United States of America states that 4-NP is a hazardous contaminant that is harmful to both humans and animals [2]. In addition to its severe toxicity to humans, animals, and plants, it has also prolonged its existence in the environment, making it difficult to undergo degradation. Thus, there is a need to fabricate easy and cost-effective analytical techniques for the selective, fast, and reliable management of 4-NP in water samples. Several techniques have been reported for the determination of 4-NP, such as high-pressure liquid chromatography (HPLC) [3], gas chromatography-mass spectroscopy (GC–MS) [4], and spectrophotometric [5]. Nevertheless, these instruments are expensive, require a prolonged sample pretreatment process, and trained personnel are needed for operation, making them inapplicable for on-spot measurements. On the other hand, electrochemical methods have been reported to have different advantages, such as cost-effectiveness, convenience for onsite application, ease of use, easier construction of electrode materials, and rapid response during analysis [6].

The surface modification of the bare-electrode using nanostructured materials is essential to improve the analytical performance, including sensitivity, selectivity, and detection limit. For instance, the AuNP nanoparticles have been used to modify pretreated graphite pencil electrodes and have exhibited improved selectivity and sensitivity with a lower detection limit (2 nmol L−1) [1]. In addition, the polycarbazole/N-doped graphene supported glassy carbon electrode (GCE) [7], and the AuNPs/RGO/GCE demonstrated excellent electrocatalytic behavior towards 4-NP reduction [8]. The Ag/neodymium oxide nanoparticles composite modified GCE [9], and the modified electrode has also shown excellent repeatability and stability for determination of 4-NP. However, these electrodes are neither flexible nor easily disposable.

Screen-printed electrode (SPE) based devices have become suitable alternatives due to their cost-effectiveness, flexibility, appropriateness for portal applications, ease of modification, as well as suitability for mass production [10–13]. In the literature, modified SPE-based electrodes have been described. For example, graphene/Nafion modified SPE was reported with good sensitivity and a lower detection limit [14]. However, the analytical features of the electrode, such as reproducibility, selectivity, and repeatability, were not investigated. Chitosan-crafted ZnO modified SPE [15] has been reported by Thirumalraj and coworkers with a dynamic range and lower detection limit. Screen-printed carbon electrodes (SPCE) have been recently explored due to the high conductivity of the carbon paste used during the printing process. However, further modification of the electrode surface chemistry is still required.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) subsist as enlarged crystalline lattices composed of transition metals with organic ligands [16,17]. MOFs are outstanding materials due to their ultra-high porosity and maximum surface area (10,000 m2/g surface area of Langmuir) [18,19], as well as superior chemical and thermal durability [20–24]. Owing to these features, MOFs have been widely used in several research domains, such as sensing [25–28], photocatalysis [29], and energy storage [30]. Amongst the various kinds of MOFs, Cu-MOF has been introduced for electrochemical sensor applications because of their high surface area, and good electrocatalytic properties [31–33]. However, the direct utilization of MOFs for electrochemical detection has the limitation of low electron transfer capabilities [34]. The incorporation of other nanomaterials into MOFs has been shown to enhance electron/charge transfer kinetics at the electrode/electrolyte interface. Doped nanomaterials with heteroatom elements including; nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), and boron (B), with the aim of enhancing the electrochemical properties of the MOFs, are becoming a growing trend among researchers [35]. Amongst these heteroatoms, N is reported to be easily incorporated into graphene [36]. The electron-donating properties and charge distribution of MOFs are improved by N-doped GO [37–39].

To the best of our knowledge, the fabrication of Cu-MOF/NGO/SPCE for the detection of 4-NP in wastewater samples has not been reported. Herein, Cu-MOF incorporated with NGO modified SPCE (Cu-MOF/NGO/SPCE) was fabricated and applied towards the detection of 4-NP as an analyte of interest.

Ferrocene, (C10H10Fe, 98%), Dimethyl sulfoxide/DMSO (C2H6OS, reagent plus purity, ≥99.5%) 1-Hexyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate/HMIMPF6 (C10H19F6N2P, ≥97%), (trimesic acid (C9H6O6, 95% purity), phosphoric acid (H3PO4, 85% purity), potassium permanganate, (KMnO4, reagent plus purity >99%), graphite powder (less than20 µM), melamine (C3H6N6), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 32%) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 35%), copper nitrate trihydrate (Cu(NO3)2·3H2O), 4-nitrophenol (C6H5NO3), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), potassium ferricyanide (K3Fe(CN)6) potassium ferrocyanide (K4Fe(CN)6), Dimethyl formamide (C3H7NO, reagent plus purity >99%), ethanol (C2H5OH, 99.5% purity) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, South Africa. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98% purity) was purchased from Merck, South Africa. Stock and sample solutions were prepared with deionized water (MilliQ water).

The single-pot modified Hummer's method was used for the synthesis of NGO [40]. In essence, 3 g of graphite powder was introduced to a mixture of H2SO4 and H3PO4 at a 9:1 ratio in a 100 mL solution. Approximately 12 g of KMnO4 was then added dropwise while stirring continuously for 30 min under a fume hood. The sample was further stirred for 22 h at 35°C to form GO sheets. Following that, 3 wt% of melamine precursor was added to the GO and constantly agitated for 30 h. Deionized water (100 mL) was then slowly introduced, followed by the addition of 30 mL of H2O2 to stop the chemical reaction. The NGO suspension was added to 100 mL of 10% HCl to remove impurities, washed with deionized water, and centrifuged until a pH of 7.0 was achieved. The yield of NGO nanomaterial was dried in an oven at 70°C overnight.

The Cu-MOF was synthesized through a solvothermal procedure [41]. In a Teflon-lined autoclave reactor, 2.5 g of Cu(NO3)2·3H2O was mixed with 1 g of trimesic acid in the mixture solution of 20 mL of DMF, 20 mL of ethanol, and 20 mL of deionized water. The solution was then heated at 100°C, in an oven for 24 h. The Cu-MOF, which is a blue powder, was recovered by filtering, washing with ethanol and DMF, and then dried in an oven at 80°C for 24 h before being dried using a desiccator.

The Cu-MOF/NGO nanocomposites were prepared via a previously published procedure [42]. Approximately 2.5 g Cu(NO3)2·3H2O and 1 g of trimesic acid with 5 wt%. NGO was mixed in a 60 mL solution containing DMF, ethanol, and DIW in a ratio of 1:1:1, respectively. The mixture was placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave reactor at 100°C for 24 h after 24 h of agitation. The remaining procedures are similar to those that were used in the Cu-MOF synthesis.

The modification process was conducted by drop-casting of nanocomposites onto the bare-SPCE. About 2 mg of the prepared Cu-MOF/NGO was ultrasonically dispersed in 1 mL of DMF to produce a homogenous dispersion. 5 μL of the Cu-MOF/NGO dispersion was drop-casted onto the bare-SPCE and dried at room temperature overnight. Finally, the dried Cu-MOF/NGO/SPCE was rinsed using deionized water to remove the unattached modifier materials. For comparison of the electrodes, Cu-MOF/SPCE and NGO/SPCE were prepared following similar producers as those for the preparation of Cu-MOF/NGO/SPCE.

The effluent wastewater samples were taken at the Pretoria, South Africa, Daspoort wastewater treatment plant, which is located at DF Malan Drive and Bazaar Street. The sample was then maintained in the refrigerator at 4 °C until the analysis was completed. Prior to electrochemical analysis, the wastewater sample was filtered through 11 μm pore size Whatman filter paper, to remove solid particles. Without further treatment, the effluent wastewater sample was taken and diluted in 5 mL PBS (pH = 7.0) for electrochemical analysis. A recovery test was carried out by spiking known concentration of the analyte into effluent wastewater samples.

The JEOL 7800F Field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) and the X-ray energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) were used to analyze the surface morphologies and elemental composition of the materials, respectively. Additionally, the surface structure, as well as elemental mapping of the nanocomposites, were studied using the JEOL JEM 2100 high-resolution Transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) coupled with EDS, respectively. The nitrogen adsorption–desorption analyzer was done using Brunauer Emmett Teller (BET). At the same time, the pore volume, surface area, and pore size of the synthesized nanocomposites were determined.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Vertex 70 model, Bruker Optic GmbH, Hamburg, Germany) was used for functional group studies. X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (Rigaku, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å), in the 2θ range of 5-90°, was utilized for crystallinity and microstrain studies. Thermal stabilities of the synthesized materials were carried out using TGA Q500 thermogravimetric analyzer (TA Instruments, New Castle, DE, USA). UV–Vis absorption spectra of synthesized nanomaterials were obtained on a Perkin Elmer UV–Vis spectrometer in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm with a slit width of 2.0 nm. The surface morphologies of the modified SPCE were evaluated using Raman/Atomic force microscopy (AFM) with WITec Alpha300 RA combined instrument.

For electrochemical experiments with a DropSens screen-printed carbon electrode, an Autolab Metrohm potentiostat, a Swiss instrument PGSTAT 302 N model, was used. Specifically, the electrochemical and impedance behaviors of electrode materials such as SPCE, Cu-MOF/SPCE, NGO/SPCE, and Cu-MOF/NGO/SPCE in 5 mmol of Fe(CN)6 3−/4− were investigated using CV and EIS, respectively. The CV measurements were performed at a scan rate of 50 mVs−1 within a potential window of -1.2 to 1.2 V. Likewise, the differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) measurements were carried out using the following parameters: potential windows (-1.2 to −0.4 V) with step potential (5 mV), modulation amplitude (25 mV), interval time (0.5 s), and modulation time (50 ms) for electrochemical detection of 4-NP.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of Cu-MOF, GO, NGO, and MOF/NGO materials were illustrated in Fig. 1 . The synthesized Cu-MOFs revealed an octahedral type of geometry and crystal structure with sharp edges, as shown in Fig. 1a, which is consistent with what has been reported by Lin and coworkers [43] . The Cu-MOF demonstrated coordinating paddle-wheel-like links between Cu (II) ions and trimesic acid organic ligands, which indicated that the Cu-MOF was successfully synthesized through Cu-metal clusters coordinated with the trimesic acid ligand. As described in Fig. 1b, the SEM image of GO indicated the formation of nanosheets. However, after doping with nitrogen using melamine as a precursor source, the GO nanosheets were distorted. This could be due to the agglomeration of NGO as illustrated in Fig. 1c, which is similar to the graphene-based MOFs reported by Chen and coworkers [44]. As shown in Fig. 1d, the SEM image of Cu-MOF/NGO indicated a flake-like/paddle-wheel-like structure. The analysis results have shown the incorporation of NGO into the porous and high surface area of Cu-MOF. Energy dispersive spectroscopy revealed the presence of all the anticipated elements such as Cu, N, O, and C in the Cu-MOF/NGO nanocomposite, confirming the interaction of the two nanomaterials (Fig. 1e).

HRTEM and EDS elemental mapping analysis ascribed a paddle-wheel-like structure to Cu-MOF as shown in Fig. 2 a, nanosheets structure of GO (Fig. 2b), agglomerated nanosheets of NGO structure (Fig. 2c), and a paddle-wheel/flake-like Cu-MOF/NGO structure (Fig. 2d). These findings indicate that FESEM and HRTEM images are in agreement and support the formation of nanocomposites. The mean particle size distribution of Cu-MOF, NGO, and Cu-MOF/NGO was calculated from HRTEM. The corresponding particle sizes are 6.68 nm, 2.19 nm, and 4.48 nm, respectively. The analysis result showed a lower aggregation size of NGO material [45] that was well dispersed onto Cu-MOF, which improved the electro-conductivity of the Cu-MOF/NGO nanocomposites.

The elemental mapping pattern verifies that Cu, O, and C derived from Cu-MOF, as well as from Cu-MOF/NGO and N derived from NGO, are all present on the surface (Fig. 2e). Moreover, for N, O, and C, uniform distributions are observed over all the surfaces. The presence of nitrogen in the Cu-MOF/NGO nanocomposite material is also confirmed. However, the Cu elemental mapping image in the Cu-MOF/NGO structure showed a dense and homogenous distribution. This confirms that the Cu2+ ion is appropriately loaded in the composite structure, which improves the catalytic property.

Fig. 4 a shows the FTIR spectra of synthesized materials. The bands of GO at 1736 cm−1, and 1032 cm−1 are attributed to the vibrational stretching of the CO, and COO groups, respectively. The incorporation of N was confirmed by an additional peak at 1600 cm−1 which is ascribed to the CN bond stretching of NGO.

The Cu-MOF and Cu-MOF/NGO demonstrated absorption peaks at 1367 cm−1, 1572 cm−1, and 1641 cm−1 which were ascribed to the carboxylate of the organic ligand stretching. Moreover, Cu–O stretching bands were found at 630 cm−1 and 1100 cm−1 in Cu-MOF and Cu-MOF/NGO, respectively. In addition to affirmation bands, the CH vibrational band of the aromatic structure of organic ligands at 723 cm−1 was observed in Cu-MOF. The FTIR features in this study are consistent with previously published research work by Mahmoodi and coworkers [47]. The stretching band at 1600 cm−1 of CN was observed in the Cu-MOF/NGO nanocomposite. From the results observed, the peaks at 1572 and 1600 cm−1 of Cu-MOF and NGO, which correspond to carboxylic and CN, respectively, are diminished as compared to Cu-MOF/NGO. This is due to the possible interaction between the carboxylic groups of Cu-MOF and the imine groups of NGO.

As illustrated in Fig. 4b, the XRD peak was observed at approximately 26° for NGO. This is mainly due to the advent of NGO disorder. Furthermore, there are NGO peaks observed at 26° (002) and 42.4° (100). XRD peaks at 6.6° (200), 10° (220), 11.5° (222), 13.4° (400), and 19.1° (440) were observed in Cu-MOF. The XRD patterns of the Cu-MOF/NGO nanocomposite also exhibited peaks at 26° (002), 42.4° (100), and 11.5° (222) with decreased intensities. As confirmed by FTIR results, there is an interaction between Cu-MOF and NGO. The presence of NGO on Cu-MOF did not alter the crystallinity shape of the synthesized Cu-MOF/NGO nanocomposite. The following equations were used to determine the crystallinity size of the materials, as well as the microstrain (1 and 2).

Where β is the full width at half-maximum/FWHM in radians, λ is the Cu-Kα radiation wavelength, which is 0.154059 nm, and θ is the Bragg angle. Table 2 lists the calculated crystalline sizes of Cu-MOF, GO, NGO, and Cu-MOF/NGO. The crystalline sizes varied from 4 nm to 29 nm. Importantly, the materials’ microstrain findings were quite low, which is directly related to the materials’ crystalline sizes [48].

As indicated in Fig. S1 (supplementary material), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) with differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was used to determine the thermal stability of NGO, Cu-MOF, and Cu-MOF/NGO. There were two weight loss stages of NGO at around 259 °C and 392 °C, which were attributed to the oxidation of amorphous carbon. At 700 °C, a substantial weight loss was observed, which was attributed to the degradation of the skeleton of N-doped graphene nanosheets. According to the TGA and DSC results, the Cu-MOF and Cu-MOF/NGO degradation began at temperatures of 82 °C and 116 °C, respectively, which is attributed to the loss of moisture. At around 400 °C, the second weight loss appeared. This suggests that the trimesic acid organic ligand has entirely decomposed from the metal–organic framework. The Cu-MOF degrades faster than the Cu-MOF/NGO nanocomposite material. This revealed that the thermal properties of the nanocomposite material were altered by the addition of NGO to the Cu-MOF.

It is very important to look at the optical characteristics of nanocomposites. The absorption band of GO and NGO showed a λmax at around 323 nm as illustrated in Fig. S2 described at supplementary material. This might be related to the n → π* shift of CO. This finding is consistent with the previous research by Peng and coworkers [49]. On the other hand, the UV–vis characteristics peak at around 500 nm wavelength, which belongs to the ligand metal charge transfer (LMCT) from the oxygen of C-OOH to the center of copper (II) in the Cu-MOF and Cu-MOF/NGO, are in line with the previously demonstrated work by Kim and coworkers [50]. Therefore, copper (II) plays a significant role in improving electrocatalytic properties [51].

The bare-SPCE and the modified SPCE were characterized using Raman spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 5 , the D band and the G band of the bare SPCE were observed at 1352 cm−1, and 1594 cm−1 respectively, which are ascribed to the carbon materials used during the ink preparation. The NGO modified SPCE electrode also exhibited the characteristic D and G bands at 1363 cm−1 and 1608 cm−1, which were red-shifted. The redshift is due to a loss of energy of scattered photons from materials, which is caused by the reduced particle size of NGO materials. Additionally, it is known that the surface energy is reduced, with decreasing particle diameter. The ID/IG ratios of the bare-SPCE and the NGO modified SPCE were 1.05 and 0.95, respectively, which indicated that the sp 2 hybridized graphite material appeared in both electrodes. In addition to these, the G-band of NGO modified SPCE was higher than the d-band, indicating that the graphite material was related to the formation of graphene sheets, and also the presence of defects was observed due to the N doping [52]. Moreover, upon modification with Cu-MOF, the D and G bands were observed at 1358 cm−1 and 1593 cm−1, respectively. As confirmed by the HRTEM results, there was no significant Raman shift, since the Cu-MOF, exhibited a high particle size compared to the NGO.

Furthermore, the Cu-MOF modified SPCE and Cu-MOF/NGO modified SPCE exhibited Raman modes at around 505 cm−1 and 834 cm−1, which were attributed to the vibration of Cu–O, and CH, respectively. The Raman modes at 110 cm−1 and 1476 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching bonds of CC and CO, respectively. However, there was an additional Raman mode at 1559 cm−1, which corresponded to the CC, due to the interaction between graphene and MOF. Wand and coworkers also reported similar Raman modes in their research work [53]. The D and G bands for the Cu-MOF/NGO modified SPCE appeared at around 1473 cm−1 and 1626 cm−1, respectively. Similarly, there was a redshift to a higher wavenumber upon the incorporation of NGO into the Cu-MOF matrix, resulting in an ID/IG ratio increase to 0.91 in comparison with the Cu-MOF modified SPCE (ID/IG = 0.8). This indicated the presence of strong electronic interaction between Cu-MOF and NGO materials.

It is evident that the incorporation of NGO increases the number of defects present in the Cu-MOF and thus enhanced electrochemical active sites on the MOF/NGO/SPCE, which are envisioned to participate in the oxidation and reduction processes with improved electron transfer kinetics.

The mean roughness (Sa) of the bare-SPCE and Cu-MOF/SPCE, NGO/SPCE, and Cu-MOF/NGO/SPCE were found to be 12.54 nm, 6.35 nm, 6.77 nm, and 6.01 nm, respectively (Fig. 6 ). This indicated that the surface smoothness of the nanocomposite modified SPCE electrode material was superior to that of the bare-SPCE [54]. The nanocomposite material, which is uniformly distributed on the surface of bare-SPCE, is expected to contribute to improving electrocatalytic performance.

The electrochemical performance of the electrode materials was investigated in a 0.1 M KCl electrolyte solution using CV measurements. As shown in Fig. 7 a, Cu-MOF/NGO/SPCE voltammograms have a larger rectangular shape of than the others. This is attributed to the synergistic properties induced by the larger surface area of MOF and the capacitive nature of NGO. Furthermore, the electrode materials were evaluated in the presence of 5 mmol L−1 Fe(CN)6 3− as a redox probe (Fig. 7b). Based on CV results, the Cu-MOF/NGO/SPCE showed an improved current response, which is twice that of the bare SPCE.

On the other hand, the performance of the fabricated electrode materials was also evaluated in ferrocene organic probes using an ionic liquid electrolyte solution as illustrated in Fig. 7c [55,56]. Cu-MOF/NGO/SPCE demonstrated improved current response, confirming similar electrochemical behavior as ferrocene and Fe(CN)6 3− redox probes. This indicated that the introduced electrode material was ascribed to an outstanding electocatalytic property towards inorganic and organic redox probes.

The effect of scan rate was investigated in Fe(CN)6 3−/4− solution at bare-SPCE and Cu-MOF/NGO/SPCE. For example, the influence of different scan rates (10 to 100 mVs−1) at Cu-MOF/NGO/SPCE was studied (Fig. 7d). The current was plotted against the square root of the scan rate, which was fitted linearly using the Randles–Sevcik equation, confirming the diffusion driven reaction mechanism at the modified electrode. Additionally, the electroactive surface areas of bare-SPCE and Cu-MOF-NGO/SPCE were found to be 0.18 and 0.22 cm2, respectively. This confirmed that the modified electrode exhibited more electroactive sites that can interact with the analyte and thus can be a suitable electrode for electrochemical sensing applications.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used to investigate the charge transfer kinetics of the electrode materials in 0.1 mol L−1 KCl (Fig. 8 a-d) and 5 mmol L−1 Fe(CN)6 3−/4− redox couple (Fig. 8e-h). As illustrated in Table S1 (supplementary material), the charge transfer resistance (Rct) and the resistance solution (Rs) of electrode materials were effectively estimated from equivalent circuit fitting (Fig. 8i) of the Nyquist plot using the nova 2.0 software. The lowest Rs values for Cu-MOF/NGO/SPCE in KCl and Fe(CN)6 3−/4− were 0.81 (±0.58%) kΩ and 0.58 (±0.71%) kΩ, respectively [57]. Additionally, the lower Rct values were exhibited at Cu-MOF/NGO/SPCE in KCl and Fe(CN)6 3−/4− were 256 (±3.39%) kΩ and 3.36 (±4.50%) kΩ, respectively. As a result, the Cu-MOF/NGO/SPCE in both solutions indicated lower Rs and Rct values than the bare-SPCE, which implies that the unmodified SPCE has sluggish charge transfer kinetics. In addition to these, the Cu-MOF modified electrode has shown higher Rs and Rct than the NGO and Cu-MOF/NGO, this might be due to lower conductivity nature of the material. Moreover, the nanocomposite materials demonstrated better conductivity, confirming that, fast electron and charge transfer kinetics take place at the electrode/analyte interface. Thus, it can be concluded that, upon Cu-MOF/NGO modification, there was improved charge transfer at the electrode/electrolyte interface due to the porous nature of the Cu-MOF and the higher conductivity of NGO.
The performance of the fabricated Cu-MOF/NGO modified SPCE towards the detection of 4-NP, as a target pollutant, was evaluated using cyclic voltammetry measurements (Fig. 9 ).

The Cu-MOF/NGO/SPCE showed a greater current response towards the 4-NP reduction than the other electrodes. The reduction reaction of 4-NP to 4-hydroxyaminophenol at Cu-MOF/NGO/SPCE produces a cathodic peak current that is approximately 3.2 times that of unmodified electrodes. This is ascribed to the porosity, good electrochemical conductivity properties, and the synergistic impact of Cu-MOF and NGO. All the electrodes indicated a cathodic peak current of −0.9 V (R1), an anodic peak current of 0.1 V (O1), and reversible redox reaction peaks of −0.12 V and 0.05 V (O2/R2). The cathodic peak current was generated by the formation of hydroxylamine molecules (R1) due to the irreversible reduction of the nitro-group with an equal number of proton and electron transfer. (R1) [58]. At high-positive potential, the oxidation process (O1) is usually associated with the electroactive species of phenol compounds, hydroxylamine, and 4-NP. Although the oxidation reaction is not required as a precondition for electrochemical analysis of 4-NP, few studies on the subject have been reported in the literature. As described in Fig. S3, the proposed reduction reaction process of 4-NP at Cu-MOF/NGO/SCPE. This proposed reduction reaction mechanism is comparable to that described in previous research papers reported by Zhang and coworkers [7].

For electrochemical detection of 4-NP utilizing Cu-MOF-NGO/SPCE, it is necessary to optimize different electrolytes and the influence of pH. Herein, 0.1 M of each electrolyte probe (HCl, NaOH, H2SO4, acetate buffer solution (ABS), and phosphate buffer solution (PBS) were used. As a result, a maximum current peak of 4-NP was obtained through PBS electrolyte solutions. Typically, different pH of PBS such as 4.0, 4.5, 5.0, 5.5, 6.0, 6.5, 7.0, 7.5, and 8.0 were evaluated for the reduction of 4-NP at Cu-MOF/NGO/SPCE (Fig S4a). The peak current was increased until pH = 7.0, and then decreased after pH = 7.0. This is due to the 4-NP deprotonation in basic media [59]. On the other hand, the potential has shifted to a more negative potential as the pH increases from 4.0 to 7.0. Furthermore, the influence of pH on the peak potential was also studied, and the results showed a linear variation of the peak potential (Ep = -0.0547pH-0.499; R2 = 0.9950) with pH in Fig S4b as described in supplementary material. The slope of 54.7 mV/pH which is slightly less than the theoretical value of 59.0 mVs−1 pH, is an indicator of an equal number of electrons and protons in reduction and oxidation reactions [7].

To examine the electrochemical performance of a Cu-MOF/NGO/SPCE towards detection of 4-NP, a CV measurement was performed at scan rates (5–400 mVs−1) as depicted in Fig. 10 a. As the scan rate was increased, the peak current of 4-NP also increased correspondingly. Moreover, cathodic peak currents of 4-NP versus scan rates were plotted with the linear equation of ipc = -17.4υ1/2 –21.7 (R2 = 0.9964) (Fig. 10b), and the log (ipc) vs log (υ) was also shown with the linear regression (log (ipc) = 0.410log (υ) + 1.48, (R2 = 0.9946) (Fig. 10b). Both findings showed that 4-NP electrochemical reactions at the modified electrode were diffusion-controlled reaction mechanisms.
The Cu-MOF/NGO/SPCE was evaluated for the electrochemical detection of 4-NP using differential pulse voltammetry.

As shown in Fig. 11 , the DPV measurements for different 4-NP concentrations were taken. At constant applied voltage (-0.787 V), the cathodic peak current/ipc increases proportionally with the increasing concentration of 4-NP in the linear range of 0.5 μmol L−1 –100 μmol L−1 with the linear equation (ip (μA) = 0.01C (μmol L−1) + 0.367, R2 = 0.9976). The limit of detection (LOD = 0.035 μmol L−1), limit of quantification (LOQ = 0.116 μmol L−1) for the DPV measurements were effectively estimated using equations (4) and (5), respectively.
(4)
LOD = 3 s d / m
(5)
LOQ = 10 s d / m

Where sd stands for the standard deviation of the blank, and m refers to the slope of the calibration. Additionally, the sensitivity was 0.045 (µA/µmol L−1) cm−2 with the dynamic range and lower LOD. This indicates that the modified electrode nanocomposite has excellent analytical efficiency. Furthermore, the performance of the developed Cu-MOF/NGO/SPCE in this work was compared with other previously reported findings, in the literature based on the linear range and LOD (Table 3 ).

The detection limit obtained is approximately identical to that of polycarbazole-N-doped graphene/glassy carbon electrode/PCZ/N-GE/GCE (6 × 10−8 mol L−1) [7]. When compared to graphene-Nafion/SPE (6.0 × 10−7 mol L−1) [14], porous copper/graphite pencil electrode/pCu-GPE (1.9 × 10−6 μmol L−1) [60], bismuth/disposable pencil-lead electrode/Bi/PLE (7.4 × 10−6 mol L−1) [62] and nano-gold/GCE (8 × 10−6 mol L−1) [63], the Cu-MOF/NGO modified SPCE has a lower LOD (3.5 × 10−8 mol L−1). The developed Cu-MOF/NGO/SPCE, on the other hand, has a higher LOD than poly(diallyldimethylammonium chloride) functionalized graphene/PDDA-G/GCE (2 × 10−8 mol L−1) [61]. The Cu-MOF/NGO is preferable in terms of simplicity of electrode fabrication, used integrated systems, cost-effectiveness, and comparable linear range.

Analytical features such as repeatability, reproducibility, and stability of the modified electrode was investigated. The relative standard deviations (RSD) of repeatability and reproducibility were 1.37% and 2.90%, respectively. It was observed that the Cu-MOF/NGO modified electrode exhibited excellent repeatability and reproducibility compared to its counterparts. In addition, the stability of the modified electrode was examined after two weeks at room temperature. After two weeks, the current response decreased by 13.5%, confirming that the modified electrode showed good durability.

On the other hand, the interference studies were investigated by adding different co-existing interference species to 10 μmol L−1 of 4-NP (Table 4 ). Using differential pulse voltammetry, about 10 fold of metal-based mixed interferences of Co, Pb, Sr, Cr, Cu, Mo, Fe, Li, Mg, Mn, Sb, Se, Ni, Ti, V, and Zn were examined. Additionally, 100 μmol L−1 of each organic co-existing species was evaluated, including phenol, bisphenol A (BPA), 4-chlorophenol (4-CP), 2,4-dichlorophenol (2,4-DCP), 4-acetamindophenol (4-AP), and hydroquinone (HQ) and 2-nitrohenol (2-NP). As a result, the interference-induced variation of the current signal response on 4-NP was less than 5%. Indicated that the Cu-MOF/NGO modified electrode has excellent selectivity towards detection of 4-NP.

The Cu-MOF/NGO/SPCE for the determination of 4-NP was examined in real wastewater samples. The 4-NP was not found due to its being below the detection limit in the wastewater samples. Therefore, the standard addition method was utilized to analyze 5 mL of effluent wastewater mixed with PBS (pH = 7.0). Aliquots of about 1, 15, and 30 μmol L−1 of 4-NP were spiked into the wastewater samples. The recovery results of 103.00% to 107.33% were obtained, indicating that the proposed sensor has the best performance towards electrochemical determination of 4-NP in wastewater samples (Table 5 ).

The Cu-MOF/NGO nanocomposites were synthesized using the solvothermal approach and characterized using several techniques. The characterization results confirmed that the Cu-MOF/NGO nanocomposite materials were prepared with the efficient incorporation of NGO into the porous and crystalline structure of Cu-MOF. The fabricated Cu-MOF/NGO modified screen-printed electrode exhibited high electron and charge transfer kinetics and hence enhanced analytical response towards the detection of 4-NP. The current signal of 4-NP revealed a dynamic range of 0.5 –100 μmol L−1 with a low detection limit (LOD) of 0.035 μmol L−1 and a limit of quantification (LOQ) of 0.116 μmol L−1. Furthermore, significantly improved analytical features such as reproducibility, repeatability, sensitivity, and selectivity were obtained. The developed electrode was successfully evaluated in wastewater samples with good recovery efficiencies (103.00 to 107.33%). These results show that the proposed Cu-MOF/NGO modified SPCE has the potential to be used as an electrochemical sensor for environmental water samples.
